Row1.Rationale: Bevacizumab binds VEGFA and prevents activation of VEGFR2‑driven angiogenic signaling; lung tumors with high VEGF pathway activity are more dependent on VEGF‑mediated neovascularization and thus more susceptible to VEGF sequestration, leading to reduced vascular permeability and tumor growth.

Row2.Rationale: Hypoxia in lung tumors stabilizes HIF‑1α, which transcriptionally upregulates VEGFA and other pro‑angiogenic genes; bevacizumab disrupts this HIF‑1–VEGFA axis by neutralizing VEGFA, impairing hypoxia‑driven angiogenesis and improving control of highly vascular NSCLC lesions.

Row3.Rationale: PI3K–AKT signaling promotes survival and proliferation in lung cancer cells and endothelial cells; when VEGF is blocked by bevacizumab, compensatory PI3K–AKT activation via alternative growth factor receptors can maintain angiogenesis and tumor viability, contributing to intrinsic or acquired resistance.

Row4.Rationale: VEGF normally activates RAF–MEK–ERK in endothelium, but MAPK can also be driven by other receptor tyrosine kinases; in bevacizumab‑treated NSCLC, persistent MAPK activity through non‑VEGF inputs sustains proliferation and migration, limiting the impact of VEGF neutralization.

Row5.Rationale: TGF‑β signaling induces epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT), extracellular matrix remodeling, and immunosuppression; upregulation of TGF‑β pathway activity in lung cancer under anti‑angiogenic pressure fosters invasive, pro‑fibrotic phenotypes that escape bevacizumab‑mediated vascular pruning.

Row6.Rationale: EMT programs increase motility, invasion, and resistance to therapy; bevacizumab‑induced hypoxia and stromal activation can enhance EMT signatures in NSCLC, enabling tumor cells to disseminate and thrive despite reduced angiogenesis.

Row7.Rationale: FGF/FGFR signaling is a key alternative pro‑angiogenic axis; in lung tumors, upregulation of FGF ligands or FGFRs can bypass VEGF dependency, maintaining endothelial proliferation and neovessel formation even when VEGFA is neutralized by bevacizumab.

Row8.Rationale: Overall angiogenic gene signatures reflect dependency on neovascularization; high VEGF‑dominant angiogenesis favors bevacizumab sensitivity, whereas a shift toward FGF/PDGF‑driven angiogenesis under treatment pressure supports resistance and disease progression.

Row9.Rationale: Bevacizumab directly reduces effective VEGFA available to bind VEGFR1/VEGFR2 on endothelial cells; disruption of this Reactome‑defined interaction network diminishes downstream survival and permeability signaling, particularly in VEGF‑addicted NSCLC vasculature.

Row10.Rationale: VEGFR2 mediates the principal mitogenic and permeability effects of VEGFA; bevacizumab lowers VEGFA–VEGFR2 activation, attenuating this pathway’s contribution to vessel sprouting and tumor perfusion in lung cancer.

Row11.Rationale: Anti‑angiogenic therapy can exacerbate intratumoral hypoxia; in NSCLC, increased hypoxia signature activity after bevacizumab may select for more aggressive, glycolytic, and invasive clones and induce additional pro‑angiogenic factors, undermining durable responses.

Row12.Rationale: TNFα/NF‑κB signaling in the tumor microenvironment promotes inflammatory angiogenesis and survival; compensatory upregulation of this pathway in lung tumors may sustain vascular support and immune evasion despite VEGF blockade by bevacizumab.
